Section 01
01

Featured project

The primary case study. A full UX system built from a business plan through research, information architecture, copywriting, visual design, and code — every phase documented and traceable back to a specific decision.

Adaptive Environment · Full UX System

Botanic Elixir

A mood-responsive café concept built as a complete UX system — from audience research and strategic framing through information architecture, copywriting, visual design, and a live coded site. Each phase is documented, connected, and traceable.

Research Strategy Writing IA & Flows Visual System Build

Section 02
02

Accessibility & input systems

Work focused on what happens when conventional interaction fails or shifts mode. Each project tests a system against its own claims and records precisely where the gap between design intent and user experience appears.

Accessibility · Voice Input

Voice Recognition Study

An investigation into how speech recognition handles punctuation, capitalisation, uncommon words, and strict formatting requirements. The artifact places system output directly against target sentences — surfacing failures with homophones like "wok," inconsistent number formats, and the difficulty of correcting errors without retyping. The study highlights that accessibility tools exist but are not always intuitive to discover or use without prior knowledge.

Speech Recognition Formatting Accuracy Language Input System Limits
View Report →
Accessibility · Screen Reader

Screen Reader Structure Analysis

Navigation of the New York Public Library site using only audio output — no visual reference. The resulting sketch reconstructs page structure based entirely on heading announcements and link titles. The NYPL's sparse alt text and unlabelled elements meant navigation relied on assumption rather than information, making the inaccessibility concrete and visible rather than abstract.

Screen Reader Semantic HTML Mental Model WCAG
View Report →
Accessibility · Tools & Transcription

Accessibility Tools & Transcription Study

Two paired studies: the first tests magnification, text scaling, and cursor configuration — examining what each setting changes and what it costs to find and use. The second is a second-by-second video transcription capturing both auditory and visual channels simultaneously, exploring what information disappears when only one channel is accessible and how much interpretive work that demands.

Magnification Text Scaling Cursor Settings Transcription Multimodal
Accessibility Tools → Transcription Study →
Section 03
03

Research

Interview critique, affinity synthesis, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthroughs, and requirements formation. Each piece uses a structured method to move from raw observation to a finding that can inform a decision.

Research · Synthesis

Affinity Diagram & Insight Formation

Interview data from three Apple Watch users — a fitness instructor, a marketing manager, and a teacher — transformed through four stages: raw extractions, thematic clustering, insight formation, and requirements reflection. The board makes the synthesis pipeline visible, showing how observations about notification behaviour, fitness tracking, and device integration become design-relevant conclusions rather than general impressions.

Interview Data Affinity Clustering Insight Formation Requirements
Affinity Diagram → Research Page →
Research · Digital Structure

Hypermedia & Hypertext — Origins & Applications

An academic paper tracing hypertext from Engelbart's NLS through Berners-Lee's creation of the WWW, examining how hypermedia principles became embedded in web architecture through REST and HATEOAS, and their continuing influence in software engineering, narrative design, and education. The argument: hypertext and hypermedia are not separate tools but underlying design principles that determine how digital information systems are constructed and used.

Hypermedia Web Architecture REST & HATEOAS Academic Paper
View Paper →
Research · Heuristic Analysis

3 Coffee Shops, 3 Cities — Heuristic Analysis

Nielsen Norman Group's ten heuristics applied to Heart Roasters (Portland), Wormhole Coffee (Chicago), and SEY Coffee (Brooklyn). Each site evaluated against specific principles — surfacing carousel feedback failures at Heart, layout scaling issues and continuity breaks at Wormhole, and typography that sacrifices legibility for minimalist aesthetics at SEY. The findings directly informed Botanic Elixir's design decisions.

NNG Heuristics Interface Critique Comparative Analysis Usability
View Analysis →
Research · Heuristic Evaluation

Heuristic Evaluation — Desert Oasis Coffee

A full NNG heuristic workbook evaluation of the Desert Oasis Coffee site — all ten heuristics assessed against specific interface moments. Issues range from branded jargon obscuring function ("Mirage Coffee Club," "Open Lab Hours") and navigation density overwhelming users, to form validation failures that allow submission without required fields. Concrete recommendations accompany every finding.

NNG Workbook All 10 Heuristics Issues + Recommendations Form Validation
View Workbook →
Research · Cognitive Walkthrough

Cognitive Walkthrough — Vera Bradley Task Flow

A six-step walkthrough of a mobile shopping task using the four-question cognitive walkthrough framework at each action — testing goal alignment, discoverability, association, and visible progress. The task traces Kim, a teacher purchasing a specific lunch cooler in "Sliced Tomatoes" ripstop on a mobile device. Findings note where the pattern filter aids narrowing and where a description dropdown adds an unexpected extra step.

Cognitive Walkthrough Task Analysis Mobile Shopping 4-Question Method
View Walkthrough →
Research · Interview Methods

UX Interview Critique & Analysis Activity

Two connected pieces. A critique of eight flawed interview questions — each identified as leading, assumptive, closed, or hypothetical, then rewritten to neutral open form. Paired with a reflective analysis of a conducted peer interview, tracing where bias appeared in the questions themselves ("Is that how you currently keep track?"), where evaluative responses shaped what the participant said next, and which probing opportunities were missed.

Interview Design Bias Identification Question Rewriting Qualitative Methods
Critique Activity → Interview Analysis →
Research · Requirements Formation

Health Requirements from User Research Synthesis

Interview data from three Apple Watch users synthesised into four structured design requirements: functional, experiential, informational, and accessibility. The reflection examines what distinguishes user-centred requirements from feature lists — noting that users wanted the device to assist other areas of life, not just track metrics, which changes what "useful" means in a design brief.

Requirements User Research Synthesis Apple Watch
View Requirements →
Section 04
04

Writing & voice

Work focused on how writing functions within a research and design process — reflection on AI support, documentation of decisions, and the distinction between using tools to think and using them to avoid thinking.

Writing & Voice · Process Reflection

AI Writing Support & Research Process Reflection

A structured reflection on how AI writing tools were used across the research and drafting process — examining responsibility, the boundary between support and substitution, and what it means to maintain ownership of academic work. The piece distinguishes between using tools to extend thinking and using them to replace it.

Reflection Writing Process AI Usage Academic Voice
View Reflection → Writing & Voice Page →
Writing & Voice · Documentation

References & AI Disclosure Documentation

A transparent record of image sourcing, AI assistance, debugging support, and implementation decisions across the project — clarifying precisely what was used, for what purpose, and where original work begins. Documentation treated as an act of clarity rather than compliance.

Documentation AI Disclosure Image Sources Process Clarity
View Document →
Section 05
05

Strategy

Work focused on how structure, positioning, and design decisions produce intentional outcomes — from a business plan and experience strategy through to a site analysis grounded in usability principles and responsive design logic.

Strategy · Experience Positioning

Botanic Elixir Business Plan & Experience Strategy

The strategic foundation the Botanic Elixir site was built from — defining adaptive environment positioning, audience segmentation, mood-based service logic, and the relationship between digital guidance and physical engagement. The plan preceded every UX decision and established what the site needed to accomplish before any structural work began.

Business Plan Audience Goals Experience Strategy Positioning
Business Plan → Strategy Page →
Strategy · Redesign Analysis

DOCR Website Analysis & Redesign Direction

A structured critique of the DOCR coffee site examining contrast failures, structural fragmentation, typographic hierarchy breakdown, and navigation that relies on scrolling without orientation cues. The analysis proposes a redesign direction grounded in card-based sectioning, semantic HTML, and a combined responsive and adaptive strategy — with code-level examples illustrating each recommendation.

Site Analysis Redesign Logic Responsive Design WCAG
View Analysis →
Section 06
06

Design

Work focused on interface behaviour, visual systems, and environment — examining how small decisions in timing, colour, and spatial sequence communicate meaning before a word is read.

Design · Interaction

Micro-Interaction Study

A review of four existing button examples — examining how feedback, timing, and visual state communicate readiness and direct attention. Paired with an original design: the label "Enter" appears letter by letter at one-second intervals after page load, drawing attention to the call-to-action while signalling that the interface is active. The gradual reveal provides feedback without requiring user input and without adding visual noise to the screen.

Micro-Interaction Button Feedback Timing Attention Direction
Design Page →
Design · Visual System

Cognitive Atelier Style Guide

A formal visual system for a cognitive environment brand — defining a four-colour primary palette anchored by Onyx and Copper, three-tier typography (Space Grotesk display, Source Serif 4 body, Space Mono system labels), WCAG contrast guidelines, and imagery principles favouring network and constellation structures over literal productivity visuals. The guide specifies that the visual system should feel like entering a designed cognitive environment, not a service marketplace.

Colour System Typography Hierarchy Usage Rules Visual Identity
See Design Work →
Design · Environment

Café Interaction Study

An exploration of ritual, atmosphere, and sensory experience through coffee preparation — examining how environmental sequence, material texture, and pacing shape perception before any explicit communication occurs. The study informs the spatial and tonal decisions in Botanic Elixir's experience design and is part of the research establishing the link between physical and digital environment logic.

Environment Design Ritual Sensory Sequence Atmosphere
View Project →

Grouped by discipline.
Connected by method.

Each section represents a distinct way of working — but the same questions run through all of them. What does this system assume? Where does the gap between design intent and user experience appear? What does structure communicate before content does? The disciplines are separated because they require different tools. The thinking behind them is the same.

Botanic Elixir Business Plan

Page 1

Business Plan for Botanic Elixir

HCI 525 · Kaitlyn Head

Purpose

“Shift your mind. Shape your experience.”

Botanic Elixir is an immersive space that moves with your intention. Whether you're deep in thought, writing your next chapter, leveling up on game night, or settling in for date night, the environment reshapes itself around your experience. With walls that adapt to your chosen purpose and drinks designed to guide your mood, Botanic Elixir is a place created for intentional living.

Page 2

Goals

This website's three goals are to create and introduce the storyline to the viewer, help visitors customize and maximize their experience, and to increase in-person engagement and community growth.

Create the Storyline

Increase viewers' understanding of the concept. Botanic Elixir is not a traditional bar, cafe, or arcade, so the site must clearly explain the adaptive environment and how the drinks work. Success is measured by deeper engagement with informational pages, drink descriptions, and experience guides.

Guide Visitors Toward the Right Experience

Because the space transforms around intention, the website needs to help guests identify whether they want to write, game, reflect, socialize, or plan a date night. Success is measured through experience-page exploration, reservations, and participation in themed nights.

Increase In-Person Engagement and Community Growth

The site should support recurring gatherings, newsletter sign-ups, and community participation by promoting upcoming themes and giving guests clear ways to plan their visits in advance.

Page 3

Themes, Titles, Keywords, and Audience

The central theme of the site is Intentional Experience Through Adaptive Design. The home screen should introduce the place while also feeling like part of the experience itself. Guests need clear paths to practical information such as the menu, hours, contact details, reservations, and hiring details, while also understanding what makes Botanic Elixir different from a typical bar, cafe, or arcade.

Titles

Home Page, Experiences, How It Works, Menu, Community & Events, Practical Info, About Us

Keywords

adaptive environment, intentional experiences, serums, mood-based drinks, creative space, writing nights, game nights, date nights, wellness events, immersive design, London, Botanic Elixir, nonalcoholic bar, experiential cafe

The intended audience is young adults, creatives, students, and professionals in London who are drawn to intentional experiences, non-alcoholic drinks, and unique social environments. Some visitors arrive for information on events, mood-based serums, and how the adaptive environment works. Others want practical details such as location, hours, reservations, or job opportunities.

Page 4

Mind Maps

The mind map outlines the core pages and features required for the site. It shows how the central sections work together to introduce visitors to the concept, explain how the adaptive system functions, guide them toward experiences that fit their intention, and provide practical information for planning a visit.

Page 5

Sketches

These sketches establish the practical information structure of the site. The layouts show how reservations, contact details, opening hours, hiring information, and a map view can coexist without losing clarity.

Page 6

Experience and Gathering Layouts

These layouts extend the wireframes into scheduled gatherings and product architecture. One page focuses on group experiments and recurring events, while the other shows how serums, room reservations, activities, and dietary information can be structured on the menu.

Page 7

Technology and Interface Structure

The technology page explains how the adaptive system works and how a visitor moves through it. It combines sequential logic with a clean presentation so that the concept feels deliberate rather than decorative.

Page 8

Current Visual Direction and Mood Selection

These visuals connect the business plan to the current implementation. The first image shows the live case-study panel and CTA alignment, while the second shows how the “Choose Your Mood” page organizes intention-based pathways across the experience.

Page 9

Storyboard

The storyboard represents a complete site structure in which every page is interconnected through persistent header navigation. The Home page functions as the central hub, allowing users to move freely between sections without being trapped in a single linear path.

Page 10

Tools, Content, Navigation, Timeline, Budget, and Growth

Tools and Code

The site will use HTML5 and CSS, with VS Code as the IDE, GitHub for version control, and analytics for measuring traffic and tracking performance against the project goals.

Content and Style

The website must help visitors understand the products, experiences, and systems that define the space. Rather than serving only as promotion, it acts as a planning and discovery tool. The visual style should feel modern and immersive, using subtle digital effects, adaptive color palettes, and refined typography to reinforce responsiveness without sacrificing clarity.

Navigation

Primary navigation is text-based in the header and repeated in the footer. Because the site includes a limited number of pages, the structure remains simple and does not require search.

Timescales and Schedule

Estimated duration: 3–4 months. The project moves through four stages: low-fidelity wireframes, clickable prototype, complete page development, and final revision and testing.

Budget

Estimated first-year cost: approximately $2,000–$3,800. This includes domain and hosting, design and development time, basic promotion, and ongoing maintenance.

Marketing and SEO

Marketing focuses on low-cost digital promotion, social media, email sign-ups, and clear calls to action within the site. SEO is supported through relevant keywords and a complete business listing with accurate hours, offerings, and contact details.

Integrated 10-page business plan with inserted images
